title: Friendship Ended with Obsidian/LaTeX, Now Typst is My Best Friend

content:
"Behold, student, and behold the place

Where you with typesetting must arm yourself."

How frozen I became—and powerless then,

Fear for yourself now, have you an ounce of wit,

What I saw, a being of life deprived.

The Spirit of College Students sorrowful

Entombed within their LaTeX plight

NixOS "broke" my Obsidian install. Instead of fixing the problem1, I instead gave up and decided to stop using Obsidian. I had heard about Typst back in March when it was first announced, and it has immediately piqued my interest—I hated LaTeX with a burning passion. I detested the backslash backslash this and that and the curly braces that and the \begin{gather} and the \begin{bmatrix} and so much more. But, LaTeX was all that I had, and I knew it well enough to not bother to switch.

Of course, I decided to switch on an impulse.

A month after having the seed of Typst embedded firmly in my mind, I decided to give it a shot for my "Foundations of Computer Science" class's last homework. If it can do the homework with some basic math and diagramming, it can do anything I need it to.

As Typst was fairly new at the point, editor support was nonexistent. As much as it pained me, I had to use their (admittedly nice) web app.

Good enough for me! After clicking through the "This is experimental! Take backups!" warning (foreshadowing!), I opened up the documentation in another window to the side and slowly pieced together my homework solutions.

What can only be described as a surprisingly pleasant and easy process led me to actually have fun with my homework! Unprecedented! I finished the assignment, threw in a few PNGs made in Draw.io or whatever they're called these days, and called it a day. Unfortunately, good typesetting cannot make up for my deficiencies in the material. Regardless, I finished the exams, passed the class, and promptly forgot about Typst for the next six-odd months.

Nix Throws a Wrench Into My Workflow

As is custom for me, I make using the computer harder for myself than it has to be. It's more fun this way, I swear. Anyhow, Nix wanted me to recompile Electron. Every. Update.

This took an emotional toll on me. A physical one as well, as I wanted to watch the progress bar go up when I should've been sleeping. This would not do.

I suffered through this for an entire goddamn month—I must've spent thirty hours just waiting on Electron to compile—before I got bored on the bus ride back home for Thanksgiving, ran home-manager switch, saw that it began to compile Electron on battery power, and decided to stop using Obsidian.

The Switch

I opened up the Typst web app, and for nostalgia's sake, looked at last semester's homework. There was a small itty-bitty problem through:

It was gone.

Not all of it, but a solid chunk of it. I know I finished the homework in Typst because I submitted a completed assignment. It seemed like the Typst web app lost a few hours of work.

I know it was in an experimental release and that it warned me to take backups, but I'm leery of cloud services anyway and the loss of work was the final nail in the coffin (even though I'm sure it's more stable now). Let's just hope that the LSP support in Vim is a lot better now…

Looks like it has improved! And to think that I wanted to spend the time and effort writing my own LSP server.

After adding Typst support into my Neovim configuration and installing Evince to preview PDFs live, I was ready to go2.

Never Goin' Back

As is now apparently custom, I decide to do the last homework of the year for my linear algebra class in Typst, but this time without a web app in the way. If you are unaware, my linear algebra homework consists of a lot of matrices. Previously, in LaTeX/Obsidian, I would have to do something like this:

\left(\begin{array}{ccc|c}
-2 & 3 & 1 & 0 \\
 1 & 0 & 1 & 3
\end{array}\right)

Which would make a matrix like this:

(−23101013)

The equivalent Typst code looks something like this:

mat(augment: #(-1),
  -2, 3, 1, 0;
   1, 0, 1, 3;
)

(Unfortunately, Typst is new enough that my CMS's code plugin doesn't yet do highlighting for it.)

The augmented matrices being easy to read is reason enough to switch. Readability went up hundredfold. Drunk on power, I rewrote the entire homework, questions and all, in Typst, just to get more comfortable with the math sections. You couldn't even pay me to rewrite the homework in LaTeX/Obsidian and make it look as good. It just wouldn't happen unless if I decided to get my hands dirty and write my own CSS for Obsidian. Which I have.

The consistency and quality of documents Typst creates are just leagues above what Obsidian does. I didn't know how much I needed soft pagebreaks until I had them. I will never let them go.

It Can't Be That Good, Right?

Of course, Typst is not perfect. I did run into a few issues outside of the workflow:

Firstly, ordered/unordered lists require indentation? It took me more than a few minutes to figure out why the numbers weren't increasing in the ordered lists. Having some kind of syntax to wrap elements as part of a bulleted section would be pretty nice, but it's kinda hard to do this without devolving into a mess of curly brackets à la LaTeX.

Next, diagramming support is still in its infancy. As tempting as it is to go all-in on Typst for my data structures and algorithms class, I may need to step outside of the ecosystem to draw red-black binary trees. Of course, I could use cetz and write custom code to draw the trees inside Typst, but that would take a lot more time than I'm comfortable with spending. Maybe when the standard library increases to have more options, I could revisit it for more than just the basics.

Finally, there are still slight inconsistencies I have noticed with Typst, especially when it comes to inline math typesetting. Notice how the font size is smaller and uncanny for the lambdas in the inline section in the matrix as opposed to the block math.

Of course, I'm being nitpicky, and I'm sure there's a way to change this behaviour (assuming that it isn't intentional, and I'm not going to join a Discord just to find out).

So Where Does That Leave Obsidian?

Typst is not a replacement for Obsidian. Obsidian is wonderful as a mind-mapping/note-taking tool. I doubt Typst can hold a candle to the note-taking abilities Obsidian has.

I, however, didn't really use Obsidian for that. Believe me, I tried—I just didn't like it. It didn't match my flow of note-taking. But for what I used Obsidian for? Typst is a phenomenal alternative.

As for where it leaves Obsidian? Languishing in the depths of my Nix store until the next garbage collection is run.

But What About LaTeX?

As much as I wish Typst will replace LaTeX, I don't think it's happening any time soon. Unfortunately, LaTeX is so deeply engrained in the spiritus mundi that it'll be around for a while. You'll get your homework papers in LaTeX, you'll get your conference paper templates in LaTeX, and all of your academically-inclined colleagues will know LaTeX. The math plugin that I wrote for this blog even uses LaTeX! Despite how much people hate it, LaTeX is still at the top, and it'll take a massive effort to dethrone it, if people even want that.

But for a project or homework that you have no collaborators for? I recommend you take the time to learn Typst. They even have a page for LaTeX users!

Who knows, maybe I'll just switch to groff or something to make it harder for myself.

Corrections? Comments? Just wanna say hi? Get in touch!

Be sure to check out the notes! I've been trying to update that fairly regularly.

I'm also back on Mastodon. Don't make me regret this.

  1. As I was writing this blog post, I figured out the problem: electron depends on prefetch-npm-deps which in turn depends on nix itself. However, I had patched the Nix package to set an environment variable when running in a nix3 shell, so this broke the chain of dependencies and thus caused prefetch-npm-deps and everything that depended on it to recompile. What caused me to look into this was that I noticed that both jellyfin-media-player and prefetch-npm-deps were being recompiled on update. Going into my flake.nix and removing the overlays section caused Electron's hashes to match, so it was only a few minutes until I figured out that the Nix patches caused the issue. Renaming the Nix package to customnix and setting the nix.package setting to use customnix fixed everything. Commit which fixed the issue. ↩

  2. After fixing an issue where the tree-sitter plugin for SQL would load (because .typ is associated with SQL) and crash Neovim because people still write software in C for some unknown reason. ↩
